:::: {.panel-tabset .nav-pills} ## Prison Population
::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Prison Population Trends

Sentence about change in admissions from 2010 to 2020.


``` ::: ::: ### Number of Releases ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Sentence about change in releases from 2010 to 2020.


``` ::: ::: ### Year-End Population ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Sentence about change in year-end population from 2010 to 2020.


``` ::: ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Admission Types

Prison populations are shaped by two primary groups: new court commitments, or individuals newly incarcerated for a criminal offense, and parole returns/revocations, those who return to prison for failing to meet the conditions of their parole. In California in 2020, people in prison for new court commitments accounted for 83% of the prison population, while parole returns and revocations accounted for 17%.


``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Who's in Prison?

``` ::: ::: ::: {.panel-tabset} ### Race and Ethinicity ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

In 2020, Hispanic, any race individuals made up the largest portion of people in prison for both new court commitments and parole returns and revocations.

``` ::: ::: ### Gender ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

In 2020, male individuals made up the largest portion of people in prison for both new court commitments and parole returns and revocations.

``` ::: ::: ### Age ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

In 2020, individuals aged 25-34 years made up the largest portion of people in prison for both new court commitments and parole returns and revocations.

``` ::: ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Sentence Length

``` ::: :::

## Parole Eligibility
::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

How is Parole Eligibility Determined?



``` ::: ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Parole-Eligible Prison Population

In 2020, there were 1,959 people were eligible for parole but still in prison for new crimes and with original sentence lengths ranging from 1 to 25 years. This group made up 2% of the prison population.



``` ::: ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

People who are Eligible for Parole

``` ::: ::: ::: {.panel-tabset} ### Race and Ethnicity ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

In 2020, Hispanic, any race people made up the largest proportion of those eligible for parole yet still incarcerated, comprising 49% of the parole-eligible population serving time for new crimes and with an original sentence length between 1-25 years.


``` ::: ::: ### Gender ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

In 2020, male people made up the largest proportion of those eligible for parole yet still incarcerated, comprising 96% of the parole-eligible population serving time for new crimes and with an original sentence length between 1-25 years.


``` ::: ::: ### Age ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

In 2020, people who were between the ages of 25-34 years made up the largest proportion of those eligible for parole yet still incarcerated, comprising 42% of the parole-eligible population serving time for new crimes and with an original sentence length between 1-25 years.


``` ::: ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Sentence Lengths

In 2020, the majority of the parole-eligible prison population serving sentences for new crimes with sentence lengths ranging from 1 to 25 years were incarcerated with an original sentence length of 5-9.9 years, accounting for 41% of this group.



``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Criminal Offenses

In 2020, the majority of the parole-eligible prison population serving sentences for new crimes with sentence lengths ranging from 1 to 25 years were incarcerated for offenses related to aggravated or simple assault, accounting for 25% of this group.


``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Missing Data

This section will have information about missing parole eligibility data for the state.
``` ::: :::

## Disparities
::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Race and Ethnicity

For every White person in prison, there are 3.1 Black, non-Hispanic people in prison.
``` ::: :::
![](C:/Users/mroberts/The Council of State Governments/JC Research - Documents/RES_Parole/data/analysis/app/ggplots/rri_infograph_Georgia.png){ width=200px }
::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
Note: The Relative Rate Index (RRI) measures racial and ethnic disparities by comparing rates between groups, often using White individuals as the reference. The RRI provides insight into the degree of overrepresentation or underrepresentation, with an RRI greater than 1 indicating that a particular racial or ethnic group is disproportionately represented. Source: BJS Prisoners (2020), Census (2020)
